data.title: Cintas Continues To Chug Along, Shares Consolidating
data.description:
We have long been a lone bull on shares of Cintas Corporation (NASDAQ:CTAS), most recently getting bullish again on the stock following its plunge in December 2024. We had called for a buy at $185 in December, and got behind the stock again a few months ago just over $200. A lot has happened this year, including walking away from its takeover of UniFirst, while focusing on expanding the core business. While that takeover would have added synergies, the price tag was eye-popping. Shares traded up another 11% or so from the last buy call, but have been consolidating in recent weeks. In this column we check back in on the performance of our house position in Cintas following the just reported fiscal Q4 earnings.

The chart suggests shares want to test $200 again, which is where we think there and below new money can re-enter. Not much has changed in terms of how this trades. Fundamentally for many on basic metrics it is "too expensive," but the growth is quite positive despite the premium valuation. Yet, there really isn't a reason why it cannot stay at a premium valuation. Many stocks do for many years. And Cintas is a leader in its niche. Let us touch on performance.

Cintas delivers a top- and bottom-line beat against expectations once again

Once again, the company delivered a double-line beat versus consensus estimates. The revenue line was a small beat of $40 million, while the EPS beat was a decent $0.02 higher per share versus expectations. Revenue was $2.67 billion, rising 8.1% compared to $2.47 billion a year ago. The organic revenue growth rate for fiscal Q4, which adjusts for the impacts of acquisitions and foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations, was a stellar 9.6%. Revenue was also up sequentially as well from fiscal Q3.

Expanding margins once again

We know that gross margins can't expand in perpetuity, however the recent trend has been extremely positive, and in fiscal Q4 we saw expansion once again. Really stellar trend here folks. Gross margin hit $1.33 billion compared to $1.22 billion a year ago, an increase of 9.1%. This was up from $1.32 billion in the sequential quarter as well, which is extremely positive. Year-over-year comps are strong and seeing sequential growth is positive. We also like that the gross margin as a percentage of revenue was 49.7%, rising 50 basis points from 49.2% a year ago. Ideally margins get and stay over 50%.

Income expansion

Cintas is still in growth mode. Operating income in fiscal Q4 was up 9.1% to $597.5 million vs. $547.6 million a year ago. Operating income however dipped from the sequential quarter, which may be why shares are reacting a bit poorly today. The operating income margin as a percentage of sales was 22.4% in Q4 2025, compared to 22.2% a year ago. This was also a sequential decrease from 23.4%. This is perhaps another reason shares were lower. But from last year we saw earnings growth. Net income was $448.3 million, up 8.2% from last year. Overall EPS hit $1.09, surpassing estimates by $0.02, compared to $1.00 a year ago. So, growth remains, but has slowed some at least in terms of the pace of growth. Still very positive though.

Dividends and repurchases

Cintas also pays a growing dividend, and is buying back shares. The company just paid a total of $611 million in dividends this fiscal year, an increase of 15.2% from the amount paid last fiscal year. While the yield is low, we love long-term dividend growth. And the company bought back $679.3 million worth of stock in the fiscal year.

Looking ahead

As we look ahead to fiscal 2026 we expect ongoing growth. The company sees revenue expanding to $11.0 to $11.15 billion, up from the $10.34 billion it brought in for fiscal 2025. We saw $4.40 in EPS this fiscal year, and for fiscal 2026 the company thinks it can achieve $4.71 to $4.85 in EPS. This assumes no acquisitions in the fiscal year, no new repurchases, and no substantial swings in the economy. From our perspective the company stock trades like a few others that are slow and steady growers, but enjoy premium valuation. There is a rich premium here so you are paying up for this growth. But the stock has long enjoyed such a premium. We think you let shares come down under $200 and do some fresh buying in $10 blocks. This is an investing approach we teach at BAD BEAT Investing. New money is best be served waiting for a little more retracement, and in this macro environment that's certainly possible.

data.title: June Retail Sales & Trade Prices: Solid Economic Data Points To A Resilient US Economy
data.description:
A trio of economic reports released this morning paints a picture of a resilient US economy with firm consumer demand and only mixed inflation pressures. Retail sales rose 0.6% MoM in June, far outpacing expectations and reversing much of May’s tariff-driven pullback. Import prices were subdued at the headline level but showed underlying pressure in key industrial segments, while export prices accelerated. Weekly jobless claims ticked lower, offering fresh evidence that labor market conditions remain relatively firm. Altogether, the data support the Fed’s patient stance, though market reaction suggested investors latched onto the softer inflation read rather than the stronger demand signals.

Retail Sales

US retail sales in June blasted past expectations, rising 0.6% MoM when forecasters only expected an increase of 0.1% MoM. This partially reversed the -0.9% MoM drop seen in May, which was the result of consumers backing away from stronger, tariff-induced spending in April. The strong end to the quarter means retail sales increased 0.5% QoQ and 4.1% YoY in Q2 2025, a signal that the consumption trend is solid. Growth was more robust when excluding gas sales, which were subdued due to low energy prices, and auto sales, which suffered from volatility due to tariff policy. This core retail sales aggregate increased 0.6% MoM in June and was up 1.1% QoQ, accelerating from the 0.6% QoQ increase in the previous quarter.

In June, motor vehicle and parts sales were a strong driver of the growth in headline sales, up 1.2% MoM. This was a bounce from the -3.8% MoM drop in May when consumers pulled back from front-loading auto purchases ahead of tariffs. On an annual basis, auto retail sales were up 6.5% YoY, and for Q2 2025, they were up 5.7% YoY. The longer-term trend that looks through the near-term volatility due to tariffs suggests there is still solid demand for motor vehicles, which is a good sign for consumer health.

Here are some highlights from other sectors:

  • Gas station sales were flat in June, suggesting a decline in volumes on the month since the BLS reported a 1.0% MoM increase in the gasoline CPI index. Gas sales had been on the decline for most of the quarter due to weak oil prices, and in Q2 2025, they were down -3.3% QoQ and -5.6% YoY.
  • Nonstore sales increased 0.4% MoM in June, building on a strong 0.6% MoM increase in May. This segment saw the strongest quarterly increase in Q2 2025 among the other goods segments at 1.5% QoQ.
  • The only segment to post a stronger quarterly growth rate was food services & drinking places, up 0.6% MoM in June and 2.5% QoQ in Q2 2025. A strong performance in this discretionary services category is a bullish signal.
  • The most discretionary goods categories like electronics & appliances (-0.1% MoM), furniture & home furnishings (-0.1% MoM), and sporting goods & hobbies (+0.2% MoM) were the weakest segments in the June report. However, they made mostly insignificant contributions to the headline growth figure because they are also the three smallest segments.
  • Building materials sales did see a solid rebound in June from the sharp decline in May, up 0.9% MoM, but broadly, the segment appears to be suffering from the sluggishness in the real estate segment. In Q2 2025, it was only up 0.3% QoQ and 0.1% YoY. Higher interest rates will continue to put downward pressure on sales related to home building and home improvement projects.

Import and Export Prices

This week featured several reports on inflation, including CPI, which looked a bit hot, and PPI, which looked a bit cool. Today’s report on trade prices presented a more mixed view on prices, with import prices seeing a smaller-than-expected rise and export prices seeing a larger-than-expected increase.

Import Prices

Import prices, which many may look at to judge how imported goods prices are trending along with elevated tariffs, saw a weaker-than-expected increase of 0.1% MoM (below expectations of 0.3% MoM). Keeping overall import prices lower was a -0.7% MoM drop in fuel import prices, the fourth month in a row of lower fuel import costs. On an annual basis, fuel import costs are down -15.7% YoY, the lowest since September 2024. While energy was a major downward force, nonfuel imports were only up 0.1% MoM, the fourth month of basically no change so far in 2025, and on an annual basis, were up 1.2% YoY (basically the same as the 1.1% YoY increase in June 2024). So, from a broad point of view, tariffs have yet to have any impact on import prices.

However, the details suggest that there are pockets of tariff-induced inflation that are not visible at the headline level. The industrial supplies & materials (excluding fuels) segment increased 0.7% MoM in June, and the more specific index tracking supplies & materials for durable goods jumped 1.2% MoM. The quarterly increases so far in 2025 for the former have also been high: 7.6% quarterly annualized rate in Q1 2025 and 5.0% quarterly annualized rate in Q2 2025. This segment tracks the import costs of key inputs for manufacturing and construction firms in the US, but with a weight of just 14.5% in the overall import index, it has a limited impact in the headlines.

Export Prices

While import prices came in soft, export prices posted a stronger-than-expected increase of 0.5% MoM when analysts forecasted a flat reading. Of note was a 0.8% MoM increase in agricultural export prices, which is the strongest monthly rise since November 2024. No-agricultural exports also saw a healthy rise, matching the headline rate of 0.5% MoM. Export price growth might be responding to an uptick in demand for US exports, especially agricultural exports like soybeans, as foreign importers might expect their countries to introduce retaliatory tariffs to counter US tariffs. Another export segment worth pointing out was the consumer goods export subindex, up 0.8% MoM, the largest monthly advance since January 2023.

Unemployment Insurance

Another minor data point that was released this morning was the weekly update on unemployment insurance. In the last two months, jobless claims started to tick higher into a trend that suggested the labor market was meaningfully loosening. This was seen in both the initial and continued claims numbers, which both were on the rise through June. But July has been a different story. Last week’s initial claims number came in at 221,000, a drop of -7,000 over the previous week and the lowest since mid-April. Continued claims were stable at 1.956 million and still up 90,000 over a year ago. Both of these data points were below expectations for this week and provide evidence against major loosening in the labor market, suggesting the strength seen in the last jobs report was not a fluke.

Market Reaction

The market reaction moved a bit counter to what one would expect in financial markets after these data points. Broadly, one would expect strong retail sales, low unemployment insurance, and a mixed trade prices report to push yields higher; however, the opposite happened. The 2-year and 10-year yields both initially spiked but ended up falling lower in the hour after the reports were released. Similarly, the DXY index saw an initial spike before softening later on. It’s possible that markets were most reactive to the cool import price index reading because it is more closely tied to the Fed’s inflation concerns, and these concerns are front and center for the FOMC.

In my opinion, the June headline import price index move should not be a viable reading of how tariffs impacted prices, and in fact, the details of the report suggested key imported nonfuel industrial inputs prices were reacting to tariffs. Combined with the retail sales beat and the jobless claims miss, the economic data today described a resilient economy, with consumers continuing to spend, which is consistent with the Fed’s patient posturing.

data.title: 3 Strong Stocks To Hedge Against Tariff Pressure
data.description:

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

August 1st is going to be here before we know it. We all know it is and there is a lot to get through today. But luckily we have Steven Cress joining us, our VP of Quantitative Strategies here at Seeking Alpha, who's going to walk you through three stocks here today that may be set up perfect for the upcoming environment.

But before we dive into that, I of course have to go ahead and get a legal disclaimer out of the way. So please bear with me one second. We are not advising you personally concerning the nature, potential, value or suitability of any particular security. You alone are solely responsible for determining whether any investment, security, or strategy or any product or service is appropriate or suitable for you based on your investment objectives and personal and financial situation. This presentation is for information purposes only. Content is presented as of the date published or indicated and may be superseded by future events. It represents my opinion, Steven Cress's opinions as well, which may not reflect the views of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, or US investment adviser or investment bank.

And I see a chat in here. Late again. Well, we get here as fast as we can. Thanks for being here, everybody.

Steven Cress: Daniel, thank you. That was a mouthful and very well said in that disclaimer. You did well. So, just by way of background, as Daniel introduced, I am Steven Cress, Head of Quantitative Strategy at Seeking Alpha. I've been at the firm for about six years. Seeking Alpha acquired my firm, CressCap Investment Research. For those who are not familiar with my background, it's a long history in quantitative research. I've been in the financial industry for over 35 years. The bulk of my career was spent at Morgan Stanley. I worked there for 13 years running a proprietary trading desk in quantitative strategies. But I also had a hedge fund that I founded in quantitative strategies as well. And as I mentioned that acquisition by Seeking Alpha of my investment research company brought me to right where I am now. And Daniel, I would say, you know..

DS: You landed at the best place. I wanted to mention that, you landed at the best place if I can put in my 2 cents.

SC: I feel I have. It's a great environment to work at, great culture, great people. And if I say so myself, I think we have awesome products at Seeking Alpha.

DS: And we've had some really great stock picks in the webinars that we've done for the last few years. So keep it going.

SC: We have. Thank you. I appreciate that. So in today's webinar, we're going to cover a little bit about the Quant system for those who are not familiar with it. Then we're going to jump into tariffs reignited. We had a little bit of a pause there with some geopolitical events in the Middle East, but it seems like we're right back in the hotspot with tariffs again. And on the back of tariffs, we're going to provide you with three recommendations that should be suitable for this environment.

So what is Quant and how do we achieve our results? Well, Quant is a data-driven process. We use mathematics and algorithms to identify investment opportunities. Quant strategies can be different. With our Quant strategy, we use what I call GARP, which is growth at a reasonable price. So it's really a combination of a number of factors. We're basically looking for stocks with strong fundamentals. And the factors that we look at are value, growth, profitability, positive EPS revisions, and momentum. And those are the core factors that we look at. And honestly, those are the same core factors that many analysts would look at who work at Morgan Stanley or Merrill Lynch or Goldman Sachs. The big difference is that we take the power of computer processing and it gives us the ability to research thousands and thousands of stocks every single day.

So literally on a daily basis, we're loading up balance sheets, income statements, cashflow statements, and hundreds of metrics for thousands and thousands of stocks. Having been an analyst myself in the past, I can tell you as an analyst, you're really only capable of maybe covering 15 or 20 companies, but through our quantitative process and the power of computers, we have the ability to use those same investment research tools and methodologies, but plug it in steroids and assess thousands of stocks. So Quant, very similar to fundamental analysis, just with a benefit of computer processing.

So we have a pretty good track record with the strategy that we employ. As I said, not all quants are the same. Some will just focus on value. Some will just focus on growth. Some will focus on momentum. We have, as I said, that combined approach. And I think that diversification is really important.

And using this strategy, what I want to show you here is basically our performance over the last five years, every single day, out of the 5,000 or so stocks that we cover, we load up all our Strong Buys, which on any given day would be 350 to 370 Strong Buys. And we measure that performance against Wall Street Strong Buys and the S&P 500. And you can see our performance has been pretty strong over the last five years. Our Quant Strong Buys are up 262% compared to Wall Street analysts up 49% and the S&P up 87%. So interesting that the S&P 500 actually beats the Wall Street Strong Buys, but thankfully our Quant system beats both of them.

Now, this is not an investable product that you're looking at. It's simply just to demonstrate that our strategy works, which is that GARP approach. And we're looking at a total of 350 plus stocks here. But there are ways that you can participate without investing in 350 companies. And we'll talk about that a little bit later.

First, I want to get into why we're here today, tariffs reignited. So, when we were back in January, February, March, April, everybody was focused on tariffs and the potential impact that it could have on inflation and interest rates. And then we hit some geopolitical events in the Middle East. And it seems like most investors forgot about the tariffs and really just focused on those events. But as of recent days and weeks, the geopolitical events are starting to calm down, and all eyes are back on tariffs again.

So, tariffs back in focus, and after a 90-day pause, which President Trump announced quite a while back, we're coming up on the end of that 90-day pause on August 1st. So as new rounds of tariffs are slated to be scheduled, and there's a lot of investor anxiety and uncertainty over that. Now, there have been some big achievements. China and the UK did reach deals. However, President Trump has threatened new tariffs on major, major trading partners, 35% tariffs on Canada, 30% tariffs on the EU and Mexico, and 25% tariffs on Japan and South Korea. And if you look to the chart that we've included here on the right hand side, you will see between the European Union, if you look at the center column for US imports, you'll see between the European Union, Canada, and Mexico, that those imports actually dwarf the imports that come from China. So that -- if these tariffs do take place, that is likely to have a major impact on many companies and consumers, potentially forcing inflation up, which would not be good.

So these moves coincide with what's already been lingering challenges for inflation. And as we're well aware of the geopolitical events that have taken place, so it's really compounding effect on top of it. And it may have actually started to have an impact on numbers recently. So specifically, if we were looking at the June consumer price index, we saw that it rose 2.7% year-over-year with core inflation coming in at 2.9%. When that number came out, the markets retreated a bit, the Dow retreated, and many financials retreated on the back of that number. So we hit market highs around July 10th. And since then, we've sort of been teetering on the top with pullbacks and when that number came out, that did not help.

There are also on top of it with the country and the European Union potential tariffs, there are product specific tariffs as well. On copper, 50%, on steel, 50%, and on aluminum, 50%. So many of these have been announced with very few exemptions. So this, in addition to the country tariffs, these sector tariffs could have potential inflationary impact as well.

So where does that bring us? It brings us into sort of a situation of turmoil and potential stagnation fears, which that is -- for the most part has been on the mind of many investors and the marketplace. The likelihood of inflation starting to tick up again and potentially unemployment rising puts us in an environment of stagflation and that is definitely not good for the market. The June inflation report did, unfortunately, it decreased the likelihood that we were hoping in September that there would be rate cuts. I'm going to show you a chart pretty soon that exhibits that. There is Fed chair uncertainty. There's the potential ousting of the Fed chair, Powell, which were two statements that came out from President Trump earlier today, earlier in the day. He was giving an indication that there could be a sign that he would be firing Federal Reserve Chair Powell, but a little bit later in the day, he sort of pulled back on that. And it was helpful when he pulled back because when that first announcement came out, the market actually did start to sell off.

So I think the market does not want to see Fed Chair Powell fired. But of course, President Trump wants lower interest rates, and that benefits the country in terms of their debt repayments, making it much lower. Of course, the Fed wants to protect against inflation and unemployment. So they're going to keep those rates higher to make sure that inflation doesn't rear its ugly head.

So as I mentioned, interest rate traders, just within a two week period, a huge change in our perspective. If you look at the right hand side, as of July 1st, 93% of the traders expected a rate cut in September. And fast forward just two weeks later to where we are now, only 56% of those traders expect a rate cut in September. So with that June CPI number and the Fed sort of holding fast to wanting to wait for more data to come out. I think the interest rate traders are saying they're not expecting a cut coming in September.

So downstream impacts, what potentially could happen here? The tariff pass-throughs, goods most exposed to tariffs include many consumer discretionary companies being furnishings, appliances, apparel. All these actually have begun to see price increases at this point. And it either gets -- those price increases come to the consumer or they come to the company. Somebody is got to pay for it. Sometimes it's a combination of both.

In terms of Q2 corporate earnings, early results in the earnings season have shown higher tariffs are compressing corporate margins with many S&P 500 companies unable to fully pass through increased costs to consumers. So that means, in many industries, the consumers aren't necessarily willing to pay up. So the companies are having to take this hit themselves. Most likely, as I said, we typically would see a combination of both the consumer having to pay more and some of it coming out of a company's profits as well.

Spillover effect, we've seen this over the last four weeks. Export-driven economies such as Mexico and Europe are already feeling the hit with stock declines and a more cautious global outlook. And really, from January, almost up until the last four weeks, we saw a lot of countries outside of the US doing well. And actually, Daniel, what I'm going to do is take us to the Seeking Alpha platform.

And this is the Seeking Alpha platform. I love this page. I probably look at it several times a day. This is a market data page where you can look at sectors or countries. And if we look at the one month return here, which you're actually going to see in the last couple of weeks, a lot of Middle East countries, the markets have done quite well. And that has been on the back of geopolitical events stabilizing there. So obviously prior to that, many of those countries were getting hit, but over the last four weeks, the markets have done very well in the Middle East.

And then when you get down to the US, you can see that had a fairly decent return, 2.83% over the last four weeks. And then when we scroll down, we're seeing a reverse of what we saw earlier in the year. Many of the returns for countries in Europe are actually over the last four weeks are now in the red. So some of these trading partners where there's potentially going to be these tariff sticking as of August 1st, those markets are actually starting to pull back.

If I click on the sectors page, you can see over the last month, technology has performed well, industrials and consumer discretionaries. If we take a look at the last five days, we could see technology has done well, but the rest of the sectors have actually pulled back quite a bit in the last five days. So I think that nice rally that we've seen in the markets it might be getting to slow down here from what we've seen in the last five days.

Taking us back to the slide deck. Outside of those countries, there are a number of industries, as you just saw, I pointed out with that sector data that are particularly vulnerable as well.

So what stocks do we want to own? Okay. If we're going to be concerned about tariffs and potential inflation, it's probably a great period to focus on companies that are very US-centric. So what we're bringing forward here, our SA Quant team and strategy, we've identified three stocks to help hedge against shifting trade policies. In addition to being Quant strong buys, these stocks display one or more of the following characteristics. One, they have domestic revenue flows that are fairly significant. Two, less reliance on global supply chains. And three, policy incentives from the Big Beautiful Bill.

So, taking us to our number one recommendation of the three stocks, ATI Inc. It's got a market cap of $12.68 billion. So it's a fairly large company. Within the industrials sector it ranks seven out of 615. So one of the reasons why we're not picking the number one, two or three again, is we're trying to focus on companies that are somewhat tariff resistant. And this company is in the aerospace and defense sector. And within that industry, it actually ranks three out of 59. They provide components for commercial and military jet engines and airframes with the overwhelming majority of its manufacturing in the United States.

In the first quarter of 2025, they showed a 9.7% year-over-year revenue increase, which was quite nice. They saw double digit sales growth in their defense area. They saw 25 -- I'm sorry, the estimate from analysts is 25% EPS growth going forward. That's a 3 to 5 year CAGR rate. That is a huge earnings per share growth rate. In fact, it's at 117% premium to the sector. They have a tremendous ROE at 25%. That's a 102% premium to the sector. And in the last 90 days, 8 analysts have taken up their earnings estimate for the company and nobody has taken it down.

Coming to our number 2 stock, Daniel, is Sterling Infrastructure. Many people who are in the Alpha Picks community might be familiar with Sterling, ticker symbol STRL. It has a market cap of $7.25 billion and a Quant Strong Buy rating. In the industrials sector it currently ranks number 22 out of 615. And within its industry of construction and engineering, it ranks 6 out of 36. 100% of their revenues come from within the United States. They have a strong domestic project backlog and recent tax reforms from the Big Beautiful Bill for infrastructure will definitely help this company out. Strong Q1 growth with 29% growth in their adjusted EPS. Talk about ROE. This one is really big. It's got a 37% ROE. So 192% premium to the sector and just a huge, huge cash per share number at $21. That is crazy. I'm actually going to take you to the platform.

DS: Not to mention you got your favorite PEG ratio on there as well.

SC: We do. Yeah. So I'm going to take you to that right now on the platform. So we're going on to the Pro platform and we're on the stock page. You can actually see the stock is up 2% today. So looking favorable. And I'm going to take you to that profitability metric. And what I'm going to show you here, actually, these are the Factor Grades. And these Factor Grades are actually sector relative. So when you're looking at valuation, growth, or profitability, you see that grade relative to the sector. So it's the industrials sector. So you know the profitability grade and the growth grade looks really good against the sector. And what's actually nice here, the growth grade itself was a C+ 6 months ago, and today it's a B+. So the growth outlook is actually improved for the company. The valuation is a D+, it was C 6 months ago. So a little bit more expensive, but still strong buy.

When I click into profitability, you will see all the underlying metrics that make up that profitability grade. And there's some great grades here, but really importantly, that cash per share at an A+. So again, it gives you an instant characterization that that cash per share is far stronger than the rest of the sector. You could see at $21 versus the sector at $2.22. And all those metrics are like this, whether you're looking at the EBIT margin or you're looking at net income margin, you could see that grade gives you an instant characterization how the metric compares to the sector. So really helpful to pick out, instead of you having to perform a lot of the research, we're doing it for you. What we do at Seeking Alpha is we actually help to identify metrics and let you know how those metrics stand versus the sector. And you're actually not going to find that on a Bloomberg or FactSet or Reuters. They provide you with the absolute data points, but we actually at Seeking Alpha interpret the data points for you to save you a lot of time in your research process.

So I'll take us back to the slide deck. And we're going to go to, and as you mentioned, that PEG ratio, coming in at 1.4x, that PEG ratio is a combination of both valuation and growth together. So it really is just a nice, neat little package to look at the two blended. And often you could see P/E that's very expensive. But if you look at the PEG ratio, it's inexpensive. And for me, that's just a great characteristic to identify both of those characteristics together.

So our number 3 pick today, Bank of America, getting away from the industrial stocks, more in the financial region here. Of course, this is a tremendous company with a market cap of $347 billion. It is a Quant Strong Buy. Within financials, It does rank 67 out of 689 and in its industry of diversified banks, it's 14 out of 66. The reason why we're picking it is 87% of the revenue -- 87% of the revenues are from U.S. comprehensive service offerings. So we are bringing Bank of America as part of that U.S. centric tariff related segments where you could get a little bit of protection. We continue to see increased concern and anxiety over tariffs. That's the whole focus of these three stocks today.

Q2 double earnings beat for them. 4% ROE growth, so that's the actual growth rate in the ROE, not the ROE itself. Net income margin at 29%. They have a whopping $4.55 billion in cash from operations and the trailing 3-month return for this stock is actually 22%. So over the last three months, this stock has performed quite nicely. If you look on the right-hand side, you'll see the current valuation grade is D, so it's getting a bit expensive, but the growth is a B-, Profitability is A, Momentum B-, and the analyst revisions are somewhat in the middle here. But you can see the performance over the last couple of months has been quite strong for Bank of America.

So Daniel, those are our top 3 choices. Here's sort of a summary page, so you could look at the different factor grades. For most of them, the valuation is a little bit expensive, but typically they're worth it because there's such strong growth and profitability and momentum and positive analyst revisions. We like these three companies as being tariff resistant stocks and they should serve everybody well.

And actually, Daniel, even if you're not looking for tariff resistant stocks, I would say these companies have great fundamentals and they all qualify as Quant Strong Buys, regardless of being tariff resistant or not.

And I believe we have an article that just came out today, focus on these three stocks plus two additional ones. So if you want to take a deeper dive into it, please either do a search on Seeking Alpha by my name and that article will come up, or you can just type in Tariffs Reignited and the article come up Best Stocks To Buy Now. And if you could, if you're not following me, you will see the follow button, just click on the follow me and you will get all my articles sent to your email going forward. As Daniel mentioned, I try to be humble, but we have a really good track record of picking stocks with the Quant system. And you'll get to see it firsthand if you do follow me.

So Daniel, that brings us towards the end. Before I get into sort of my end, do you have any questions that you want to highlight?

DS: No, I was just going to say I'm the hype guy here. I've got to make you, I got to hype you up a little bit. I mean, your track record has been great. Everybody, I can't encourage you enough, follows Steven Cress’ author profile here on Seeking Alpha. They put out articles all the time, even sometimes a week. I mean, it's just incredible how much content you're putting out there for everybody, Steve. And specifically, at moments like this, where we have tariffs coming down the line, there's some questions in here, we'll get to about, the upcoming stuff about pharma and everything else, because there's - we haven't even gotten all the tariffs on that yet. So there's a lot of stuff coming down the line, especially during these volatile markets, you're always a nice sound voice.

Before we dump into PQP, I do have one question for you. And it's the other ones afterwards. People look at the three stocks that you just presented, and they go, well, Steve, these stocks, the share price are near the all-time highs. Like is this actually the right time for these stocks? What would you say to that?

SC: Yeah, I would say you shouldn't really determine if the time is right for a stock by looking at the 52-week high or the 52-week low. That's not really an appropriate way to value the stock. When you want to look at if a stock is an appropriate Buy or not, you have to look at its entire framework. That's the valuation framework, the growth framework, the profitability framework, and you want to look at it versus its peer group. And that's what tells you if a stock is a Buy or not.

So if we go back in the presentation, or actually I'll just go to Sterling, and I go to the summary page, you could see Sterling is near a 52-week high, but you could basically say, you know, from this point in time here, when it was like $188, it was near a 52-week high, $195, $205, $204, $220, $228, $243 to where we are now, you would have missed out on all that upside. If your perspective was, I was afraid to buy at a 52-week high. So that should not influence anybody whatsoever.

Take a look at the valuation, see where it is versus the sector. For a stock that's got a valuation of a D or D+, that could still qualify as a Strong Buy for us. If it's a D- or F that would not qualify, that would default to a Hold. But you're looking at growth versus the sector that's stronger, probability versus the sector, that's stronger, momentum versus the sector that's stronger, and analysts taking up their revisions. So the actual quantity of analysts that are taking up their earnings estimates is stronger versus the sector as well. So these are the important factors that you want to look at, whether it's at a 52-week low or high that shouldn't determine it. But I will tell you, if you were to take a basket of 10 stocks that were at a 52-week low versus a basket of stocks that was at the 52-week high, you'd be far better off buying stocks that were at the 52-week high than the low. Because at a 52-week low, it's most often going to be a value trap and you don't want to get stuck in that trap. So Daniel, back to you.

DS: Well said. All right. So let's talk about the Pro Quant portfolio here.

SC: All right.

DS: Maybe some people know about this already, maybe some people don't. So let's go ahead and start from the beginning.

SC: All right. So I developed a new product about a month and a half ago. We launched it on June 1st. And as I mentioned earlier, we have a really good track record. Our concept of our GARP strategy has worked very well. But when you look at our track record, we're looking at about 350 Strong Buys, and that's not feasible. But what we decided to do is create a product where we're really focusing on our top decile of stocks in the Quant system. Because actually, if you just looked even at the Strong Buys and you broke that down to deciles, the top decile has better performance than the bottom decile.

So we developed this system where we're going to have a fixed portfolio of 30 stocks, and we're going to rebalance that on a weekly basis, which will provide our users with anywhere from one to maybe three new ideas a week. Some weeks there’ll be no new ideas. Others there’ll be three. Sometimes it's just one. And that means as one name comes in, one name will go out. But we performed a back test. And the back test, which went to 2015 through May of 2025, absolutely crushed the S&P 500. We did it on an equal weighted basis. Since we have a fixed portfolio of 30 stocks and we're rebalancing it every week, we decided to use the S&P on an equal weighted basis and not a market cap weighted basis. They're still fairly close, but irregardless, our strategy completely crushed it.

So it is a data-driven process. It's using our Quant model and the portfolio itself is a systematic process. So for the most part, we're going with our top quant picks. We're letting the computer select the stocks. There is a human override. So if something were to be a little bit off, I would be able to observe it and make changes. But for the most part, this is a systematic model using our data-driven process. And as you can see, what we try to do is remove emotion from investing. And that's why we follow the data. And just using this strategy, it has worked out extremely well. So, Daniel, this is the back-tested performance.

The next slide I'm going to show you, are the actual results that we've had from May 30th to June 16th, about a month and a half. And you can see the PRO portfolio in that very short period is up 16.87% versus the S&P on an equal weighted basis of 3.26%. If you look at the S&P below, we actually took the S&P on a regular market cap weighted basis. That's up about 5.62%. So outperforming the S&P on an equal weighted basis, but still the PRO Quant Portfolio, absolutely crushing it with its active return here of 16.87%.

So if you don't want to do the research on your own, if you would like a product where we're giving you our top 30 stocks, and we are paying attention and we're rebalancing on a weekly basis, So you can have those fresh ideas and know what to buy and what to sell, the PRO Quant Portfolio is probably the place to be. And Daniel, maybe you could tell them a little bit about the pricing for the PRO Quant Portfolio.

DS: Yeah. So this is the exciting thing is PRO Quant Portfolio, obviously, for the people that are here that are already PRO subscribers, you have access to this portfolio right now. If you are Premium or Alpha Picks, this is an additional subscription. You can always reach out to our customer support team if you are a Premium customer and have the rest of your membership credited to PRO, if you feel like joining. The great thing here is that for the first 30 days of using Seeking Alpha PRO it’ll only costs you $99. So that is a huge discount to the overall price. You can get in, you can see the PRO Quant portfolio, see what it's all about, see the upcoming stock picks, the upcoming stock sells as well. And hopefully, we really hope that you have some really great success utilizing this product.

Obviously, you've seen the back test, you've seen this PRO portfolio success of the system approach as of right now. Now I will say as well as a disclaimer that this is a system, right? So you're thinking about it, a computer is faster than a human. So it is not going to be a 100% the same results for you probably than the system because a computer can trade a lot faster than we can. But I encourage everybody to check it out. I mean, the community is growing there. Everybody is engaged in the analysis and the comment section as well, just like if you're an Alpha Picks subscribers, it's the same kind of deal. And the team is always here ready to help. So hopefully that helps.

I'm like, oh, and if the team in the back can go ahead and drop the link in the chat for everybody to check it out, we'd appreciate that.

SC: All right, and I think just to sort of finalize it, you may be aware there's another product that we have that's called Alpha Picks. So I just wanted to provide you with a couple of the differences. As I mentioned on the PRO Quant Portfolio, it is 30 equally weighted positions. With Alpha Picks, we introduce two new ideas a month. So that portfolio can actually be far larger than 30 stocks. It could be 40, 50, or 60 stocks at any given time. With the PRO portfolio, it will always be 30 stocks, unless we're sort of mid-week and there's a Sell. So it would drop down, but on the rebalance, it would bring it back to 30 stocks. And that rebalance occurs every Monday at 9:30. With Alpha Picks it is on the first of the month or the 15th of the month or the trading days closest to that point. The stock universe is much larger for the PRO Quant Portfolio because we're looking basically at all ADRs, all market cap levels, all U.S. securities. With Alpha Picks, the market cap has to be above $500 million. And it's predominantly US stocks, the only ADRs would be primarily listed USADRs. There's not many of those.

The share price minimum for Alpha Pick, we're not going to pick stocks that are under $10. There is no share price minimum for a PRO. Turnovers definitely got a higher frequency with the PRO portfolio than it does with the Alpha Picks. So you have to decide do you want a full portfolio of 30 stocks. And do you want that rebalance on a weekly basis where we're letting you know what the next best Quant stock is. Or if you want something at a little bit of a lower pace and you're happy just getting two ideas a month, Alpha Picks would be the right product.

And Daniel, I think that's it. If you have any more questions, I'm happy to answer them.

DS: Yeah, we'll jump into the questions. I do want to clarify as well. I mean, PRO Quant Portfolio, 30 stocks, you may not want to own every 30 stocks, so like all 30 stocks, I mean. So if you want to check out the product and maybe pick a few of the stocks, or maybe pick some of the new picks that come out on Mondays, that is another way to leverage it as well. You can learn more about it. There was a link I saw dropped in the chat to a webinar that Steve and I did more about Pro Quant Portfolio. If you're watching the replay, that link will be below this video as well. You can dive and learn more about the portfolio and how you can best utilize it for the Pro Quant Portfolio. And again, that's $99 for the first month.

All right. So let's dive into some questions, Steve, about these stock picks here today. So give me one second. There was a question. You showcased the sectors and healthcare, pharma, things that's going on in the yields and the interest rates. People were kind of wondering, what are you kind of anticipating or maybe what the data is kind of showing us about what might be happening in the healthcare sector going forward. I don't know if you want to go back to the sector page on the platform and look at it or not, but…

SC: Yeah. Well, what I can tell you is, historically, people view the healthcare sector as more of a defensive sector. It's not like the consumer discretionary sector where it could be a random purchase that you're making. When people get defensive, they look at consumer staples, they look at utilities, they look at REITs, they look at healthcare. Specifically within healthcare, they look at the big pharma stocks. So it tends to be more defensive in nature. Of course, with the tariffs, there could be huge tariffs on some of the foreign companies and the pharmaceuticals. So that could have an impact on pharmacies here in the U.S. that have to buy those drugs. So there's a bit of uncertainty in the tariff world, but for the most part, people will need their medicines. And that's why it tends to be more of a defensive sector because it's a must have purchase. It's not a discretionary purpose.

DS: We have a question here saying of the three stocks given today, do you have a price target for these three stocks?

SC: I do not have price targets on any stock. And the reason why is I really don't believe in price targets. You'll see a number of the brokerage firms and their research reports will typically put price targets out there. And I think they really use that as sort of an enticing feature to let people know that there's a lot of upside or minimum upside, but really stocks trade every single day. The world changes every day. The macro environment changes every day. The industries change. And as such, it's a moving target. And I can almost guarantee you, whenever a stock hits its price target, if the stock's doing well, the analyst is going to move the price target up. So we look at a company's valuation framework and growth framework versus the sector. And we use those factor grades and those factor grades give you an instant characterization of where it is versus the sector. And we use that as our guiding basically to determine if you want to stay in a stock or not. We don't use price targets.

DS: We have a question here that says, what if there are no tariffs imposed? Are these still worth considering as a Buy? So I think the big question is, do you think tariffs are actually going into effect? I mean, and they say, they have some tariff revenue already going on coming into America.

SC: It's starting to happen. Yeah. So, yeah, tariffs are inflationary. But I think to answer that question, I think, irregardless of the tariffs or not, these are great companies to own. So I think the fundamentals are really strong. The financials are really strong. The growth rates will still be strong, irregardless of tariffs. So I think if the tariffs do come, I would want to own these stocks. But even if the tariffs did not come, I would still want to own these stocks.

DS: All right. We have, how long does it take before the Quant rating drops when a stock continues to drop day-after-day?

SC: Great question. So that would definitely impact momentum. So we look at momentum over four different time periods. We look at 12 month momentum, nine month, six month, and three month momentum. So we're looking at all four points, but at a certain point, if it does have an impact on two to three out of the four, it will have an impact, that Quant momentum grade will come down and a stock can drop from a Strong Buy or Buy to a Hold. So momentum is definitely an important factor that we look at. And actually, of all the metrics, and you're looking at literally hundreds of financial metrics between value, growth, and profitability, the empirical data shows that momentum is actually the most important factor, and that data goes back over 200 years. So momentum is a really important factor in terms of price predictability for stocks.

DS: Steve, we have a really valid question here. How do I know when to Sell these stocks if I add them to my portfolio?

SC: Very valid question and we make it super easy for you. If you see that Quant rating change to a Sell or a Strong Sell, you know it's time to sell.

DS: All right. Quick and easy.

SC: Yeah. Hold does not mean Sell, Daniel. Hold definitely does not mean Sell.

DS: That's the key part. Right. Hold does not mean Sell. All right. Give me one second. And there are a lot of questions here. All right. So given that each investor has a unique risk profile and portfolio strategy, how would you recommend approaching the allocation to stock recommendations presented here today?

SC: We have three stocks that we're recommending here today. And I think that the individual highlights a very important point. We have no idea what your risk tolerance is. We have no idea what your capital level is. But I will say, I am a big believer in diversification. I'm a big believer in dollar cost averaging. I think that helps basically create generational wealth over time. I often tell people not to pay attention to the talking heads. Don't worry if the market is going up or down, stick to a discipline where you continue to buy names in month-in and month-out. And really it's like at those months, it doesn't feel like it at the time, but when we're having the biggest pullbacks in the market, that's when you're actually going to make your most amount of money. So we've actually have done an analysis going back to 2010, the market corrected 5x at a level of 15% or more. And if you want to just -- in some periods it was far more than 15%. But if you just executed at that point where the market was down 15%, whether you, if you bought the S&P at that time, if you held it for two years, you are up to close to 50% on average over those instances. And if you bought our top 10 Strong Buys at that point where the market had a 15% pullback, you're up on average 117%. So point being, those pullbacks are your best friend for making money. So don't be afraid of it. Just look at it like everything's on sale and you're buying your favorite stocks on sale.

DS: Well said. Steve, they want to know what do you think about gold in this environment?

SC: Well, if there is going to be inflation, gold is a good place to be. So really, again, it's hard for me to determine what your risk tolerances are, what your thoughts are on inflation. But if you do believe that we are going into an environment where there will be tariffs, if you do believe that interest rates could be going higher, gold is still probably a good place to be.

DS: All right. I'm going to go ahead and answer two quick questions that came in here. So if you are an Alpha Picks subscriber and you want to join PRO, that is a separate subscription. If you are Premium and want to level up to PRO, you can get the remaining balance of your Premium subscription moved over to your PRO account. Just reach out to subscriptions@seekingalpha.com and they can do that for you. There was another question about Pro Quant Portfolio where there's buying and selling each week. It's also worth noting, Steve, that not every week has a Buy and a Sell, right?

SC: That’s right.

DS: Portfolio systematically works. It's all based off of the Quant system. But with buying and selling, you do get that analysis article that comes out at 09:30 A.M. Monday morning when the market opens, telling you what was bought, what was sold, and how the stock values are reweighted in the portfolio for the upcoming week. So we just wanted to clarify that.

Let's see. What do you think about industrials? I guess we've talked a lot about industrial stocks lately, PLOW, PSIX, in particular. What are we thinking about the industrial sector within the upcoming tariff environment?

SC: Well, for me, the way I look at it is that there's going to be some major infrastructure build out. And whether we're coming off of the infrastructure bill that became a law in the last administration and with what President Trump is introducing, it all spells out to be pretty good for infrastructure in the United States. So between infrastructure and between power demand from a number of different places, whether we're looking at AI or EVs or just a very old utility industry, there's going to be a tremendous demand for upgrading that infrastructure. So I think the industrial companies stand to do really quite well over the next couple of years. So I'm a big fan of industrials here.

DS: All right. There's actually a question that says, does Hold mean Buy? That came from Chris.

SC: Does Hold mean Buy? Hold means Hold. So often what it will happen, I will tell you, since this is a data driven process, you have to think of it in terms of there's going to be a point where almost, like, if you're looking at academic grades in a scoring system, there's a certain point where you go from an A- to a B+, but we have those same triggers here. There's a certain point where it gets triggered, but with a good student, sometimes they can go back from B+ to A -, and it can happen in a very quick period. So that's why when something goes to Hold, we mean really hold onto that stock and wait. By an example, in the Alpha Picks portfolio, Daniel, when we have a stock that drops from a Buy to Hold, we actually keep it in the portfolio for 180 days before we take it out. If it's a Hold for 180 days at that point, we'll remove it and make room for some of the new Strong Buys.

In the Pro Quant Portfolio, we don't kick a Hold out right away. We keep it in there. It's a limited amount of time that we keep it in there. But in both portfolios, we'll keep a Hold. We do wait to see if it flips back. So we don't believe that a Hold is a Sell, and we tell people to hold on to their Holds.

DS: There's a really interesting question here. We haven't gotten one of these like in a while so I'm going to ask it to you. This is from Samuel, says, how does the percentage of float short affect the Quant system if at all?

SC: I will tell you, we have done, personally, I've done a lot of back testing. I can't say that Seeking Alpha has done a lot of back testing, but in my 35 years and focusing a great amount of that on quant strategies, I've done a number of back testing on shorts and it actually is not a good predictive indicator. I would not use that as an indicator. And in fact, sometimes I will tell people if there is a high short interest and it's a Quant Strong Buy, chances are you actually might see a short squeeze on that, which could even force a stock higher, but it is not a good predictive factor. So we really do not weigh that.

DS: All right, Steve. Do me one favor real quick before we get on out of here. Go ahead and throw up that slide of the three stocks that we covered here today, if you don't mind, just so that everybody can remember what we went through today. Hey, glad you're finding the webinar very informative. There are a ton of people here, Steve. I hope everybody learned something today. There's a lot of positive feedback here. Obviously, the three stocks that we talked about today, ATI, STRL, and Bank of America, BAC. Now, that's an interesting one too. When you think about the banks and potential rate cuts down the line, kind of, how do you think about that actually? If there's a rate cut, how do you expect financials to react?

SC: Well, diversified financial could react a lot differently than a payments financial company. Right now, mortgage rates have moved off of their lows from where they were in the pandemic. So obviously, they're making more there. If commercial entities need loans, it's at higher interest rates. So they're actually making a little bit extra money right now. So the stocks for the most part of the financials have done fairly well since the period of the Silicon Valley crisis that occurred. Almost if you bought during that period, you've made out like a bandit by holding onto the stocks or buying financials since then. The reason why we highlighted Bank of America is because I think, as I mentioned, like, over 85% of their revenue is U.S. based. So that's why we wanted to throw that in there and have something a little bit different compared to the two industrial stocks. But if interest rates were to come down, I would not expect dramatic cuts here. So I don't think there would be a huge impact on the financials.

DS: Yeah. I just checked it on the sector page, the financial sector up 6.87% year-to-date as of right now. Pretty interesting. All right, Steve.

SC: Yeah. The last month has been a good month. We've had a good four week period for the financials.

DS: But tariffs are coming and we will see where we go from here. So Steve…

SC: Maybe we'll see. We'll see.

DS: Can’t thank you enough for your time as always. Everyone, thank you for taking the time to either watch the replay here or join us live today. If you haven't, check out Pro Quant Portfolio, you'll hear us talking about it a ton going forward and we'll keep you updated on the results so you can see it. But again $99 for 30 days. So I hope you get to try it out and check it out for yourself, figure out how it may benefit you. All right, everyone, and that'll wrap it up for us. Have a great rest of the week. Be careful out there and we'll see you in the next webinar.

SC: Thank you.

data.title: BBJP: Yen Declines Generally Benefit Japan, But Tariffs Hit Most Allocations
data.description:
The JPMorgan BetaBuilders Japan ETF (BATS:BBJP) is a lower expense ratio ETF than the iShares MSCI Japan ETF (EWJ) despite a more particular mandate at 0.19% compared to 0.5%. The differences in exposures are negligible in terms of sector, so we strictly prefer the BBJP over the EWJ. It is also better in exposures to the more value-focused Japanese indices like the iShares MSCI Japan Value ETF (EWJV) due to the greater exposure to IT, where value ETFs favour more exposed industries like financials and heavier industry, affected by a weaker Yen and tariffs. But even within IT, Japan has more exposure than most to hardware which will still be at risk of tariffs. The benefit of the bulk of Japanese companies in the broader index to the possibly weakening Yen due to election and fiscal uncertainty will likely be offset by the scale in change of competitiveness of Japanese export to the crucial US market, accounting for about 20% of their export. Tariffs are going from negligible in the US to possibly 25% absent a trade deal. While a weakening Yen, though it benefits across all end markets by geography, will likely not weaken to that extent - and the US typically commands the bulk of the geography mix in Japanese stocks, reflected in the economy's overall high export ratio to the US. Keep in mind also that other export led countries such as many in Europe are also being affected by tariffs, and Japanese products will have to compete more fiercely on an incremental basis in the remaining markets with those reconfigured flows.

In our experience Japanese companies are more sensitive than in the US to QoQ/YoY changes in earnings, in part due to the more scarce explanation of longer term business trends and speculated positives on the horizon, where US managers speak more optimistically and do more to guide on business direction. This is another reason to be concerned about broad Japanese exposures where tariffs have not yet factored into stock results, with latest results mostly being FY figures ended March 2025.

BBJP Breakdown

Above are the sectoral exposures for the BBJP. The big ones are Industrials, with the major trading houses, including Hitachi (OTCPK:HTHIY) among others. Then there is consumer discretionary dominated by automotive companies. Then there are financials and finally there is IT, including service companies like Recruit Holdings (OTCPK:RCRRF), but also other hard industry like the production of semiconductor manufacturing equipment and semiconductor components.

IT is a bit of a bright spot. With a decent amount of service exposure, for example Recruit at 1.87%, there is no tariff risk and a lot of business is coming in the form of USD, which protects it from the Yen risk that is stemming from possible fiscal implications of a speculated change of prime minister in Japan that may be more prone to stimulus and harm demand for JPY denominated bonds on a deterioration of government finances. On the other hand there is a lot of heavy industry here. Tokyo Electron (OTCPK:TOELY) has quite a big US market. These sorts of products will get tariffed presumably. While a possibly weakening Yen, exacerbated by the threat of a reignited Yen carry trade with JPY debt rates still low and the Yen decline a self fulfilling prophecy, ordinarily helps the export-laden Japanese market the benefits of a somewhat weaker Yen could be more than neutralised in terms of competitiveness by the significant tariff rate jump from 10% to possibly 25% absent a deal. The same argument goes for consumer discretionary (mainly cars) and industrials, where the whole Japanese economy is highly export dependent and also on the US.

Bottom Line

The weaker Yen negatively affects businesses whose flows are entirely coming from the domestic market like financials. There is no benefit here in terms of competitiveness of export and the value of that Yen income falls for ex-Japanese investors. Financials typically at least hold a decent amount of USD-denominated debt in Japan, but the market in Japan is influenced by earnings flow, and it's not a good thing when the income currency becomes devalued. Financials also own massive amounts of the Japanese government debt. With the majority of the Japanese market not having reported yet results including the effects of even the initial 10% tariff, we have some fears about the coming Japanese earnings season and expect some pressure on the equity markets. Moreover, the climbing rates on the worsening outlook for Japan's financials on account of the prime minister change speculation has a negative cost of capital implication. Higher multiple ETFs/companies will be more affected, compared to lower multiple Japan ETFs like the EWJV at 11x, as opposed to the EWJ/BBJP at around 15x. Japan is not a major danger market as there is some natural hedging going on when the Yen weakens on the export stocks. The Yen seems to be weakening given the political speculation compounded by carry trade short fears around the JPY. The IT exposure also contains service stocks, unaffected directly by tariffs. We want to be more selective than the ETF when approaching Japan, trying to go for clearer winners than US-export heavy businesses that will likely see a net negative effect from tariffs. Though we are worried about a renewed bear move for the Yen due to the perniciousness of the carry trade on Yen dynamics. The mitigating factor here is that the reflection of the weakening Yen on fiscal fears will be in higher rates, which slightly dissuades borrowing in Yen and hence the carry trade.

data.title: U.S. Jobs And Spending Numbers Suggest Little Need For An Imminent Rate Cut
data.description:
By James Knightley

Firmer retail sales and subdued jobless claims numbers suggest the Fed will keep rates on hold for now as officials assess the impact of tariffs on inflation

June retail sales gains amidst consumer caution

Today's US data is generally on the firmer side in terms of activity and jobs, while import prices are a touch softer than anticipated. Overall, it supports the view that there is little pressing need for another interest rate cut from the Federal Reserve.

Retail sales rose 0.6% month-on-month in June versus expectations of a 0.1% gain. We tend to focus more on the 'control group', which strips out volatile components such as autos, building materials, gasoline and eating out, as this tends to better align with broader consumer spending trends (including services) and therefore gives us a better guide to consumption within GDP. That rose 0.5% MoM versus 0.3% expected, but May's growth rate was revised down to 0.2% from 0.4%, leaving us broadly in line with expectations in dollar spending level terms. We need to remember that retail sales is a nominal dollar growth rate, so in real, inflation-adjusted terms as GDP is measured, we need to knock off around 0.2 percentage points from the growth rate given what happened in the CPI report.

The details show strong nominal dollar auto sales growth of 1.2%, which contradicts the fall in volume numbers released by the automakers (15.34m units in June versus 15.65m in May) and the supposed fall in prices reported within the CPI report (-0.3%). Building materials also rose sharply, which is perhaps preemptive buying ahead of tariffs, particularly with copper in the news. Meanwhile, 'miscellaneous' jumped 1.8%. Non-store retailers (internet) saw modest sales of only 0.4%.

Retail sales account for around 42% of total consumer spending, and as the chart below shows, the trend in 2025 has been a noticeable flat lining after strong post-pandemic gains. Consumer sentiment has softened significantly this year as households worry about the impact of tariff-induced price hikes hurting spending power, a cooling jobs market, and major swings in household wealth.

US real consumer spending levels (Dec 2024 = 100)

Jobless claims still hint at a softening jobs market

Jobless claims dipped a little further to 221k from 228k. The improvement here may be tied to the timing and scale of auto retooling shutdowns, which can prompt volatility over the summer months, given the cooling in hiring surveys and warning of rising layoffs in manufacturing. So the risk is that this moves higher once again over late summer. Continuing claims rose to 1956k from a downwardly revised 1954k (initially reported as 1965k). The upward trend in continuing claims reinforces concerns about the cooling labor market and the challenges of finding new work if you lose your current job.

Continuing jobless claims highlight the cooler jobs market (millions)

Import prices suggest tentative willingness of foreigners to lower prices in response to tariffs

As for import prices, the June figure came in at +0.1% versus +0.3% expected, but May's figure was revised down to -0.4% from 0.0%. This is important as it perhaps suggests some willingness from foreign manufacturers to absorb part of the impact from tariffs via lower pricing. However, these are pretty small moves set against substantial tariff hikes and suggest that American businesses and households will bear the brunt.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post
